subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Balance of Power Between the President and Congress
stimulus: The power of the executive branch in relation to the legislative branch has varied over time. Develop an argument that takes a position on the appropriate balance of power between the president and Congress.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Declaration of Independence, The Federalist 51, The Federalist 70.)
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a position on the appropriate balance of power. Restating the prompt or simply noting that powers vary over time does not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The power of the executive should be strengthened relative to Congress because Congress is slow to react and sometimes ends up in gridlock.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The power of Congress should be strengthened relative to the president because Congress is the representative of the will of the people.”

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: “The power of the executive and legislative branches should be equal because no one branch should become too powerful/limited government.”

wrong_examples: “The power of the executive and legislative branches of government are important because there is a balance of power.” Do not respond to the prompt
“The executive branch is the most effective branch of government because the president has many constitutional powers.”
common_traps: Restating the prompt without making a claim
Describing the issue without taking a position

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from the Declaration of Independence, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (0 points): No accurate evidence or evidence not relevant to the topic.

required_elements: No accurate or relevant evidence
alternatives.correct_example: No points earned — evidence is inaccurate or irrelevant to the prompt.

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: Provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: May or may not have a claim or thesis.
alternatives.correct_example: May or may not have a claim or thesis.

common_traps: Evidence must be relevant but need not directly support a specific claim to earn 1 point

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim/thesis OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence.

required_elements: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.
alternatives.correct_example: Provide one piece of specific and relevant evidence that supports the claim or thesis. This evidence can come from one of the foundational documents listed in the prompt, any other foundational document, or from knowledge of course concepts.

common_traps: To earn 2 points, the response must have earned Row A (claim/thesis)

scoring_points.description: Row B — Evidence (3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim, one from a listed foundational document.

required_elements: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific
alternatives.correct_example: Provide two pieces of specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis. One of these pieces of evidence must come from a foundational document listed in the prompt. The other piece of evidence can come from a different foundational document or from knowledge of course concepts. Examples that do not earn points: Provide evidence that is not specific

required_elements: “The Constitution establishes the legislative branch.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt
alternatives.correct_example: “The Constitution establishes the legislative branch.” Provide evidence that is not relevant to the topic of the prompt

required_elements: “Civil liberties are protections provided to citizens against the actions of the government.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:
alternatives.correct_example: “Civil liberties are protections provided to citizens against the actions of the government.” Examples of evidence that are relevant to the topic of the prompt:

required_elements: War Powers Act
alternatives.correct_example: War Powers Act

required_elements: Impeachment
alternatives.correct_example: Impeachment

required_elements: Veto with super majority for override
alternatives.correct_example: Veto with super majority for override

required_elements: Ratifying treaties
alternatives.correct_example: Ratifying treaties

required_elements: Confirmation of presidential nominees
alternatives.correct_example: Confirmation of presidential nominees

required_elements: Federalist 10
alternatives.correct_example: Federalist 10

required_elements: Articles of Confederation
alternatives.correct_example: Articles of Confederation

required_elements: Any correct check between the executive and legislative branches Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):
alternatives.correct_example: Any correct check between the executive and legislative branches Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):

required_elements: “The actions of the executive branch during the Vietnam War showed continued encroachment upon legislative war powers, therefore, Congress passed the War Powers Act in 1973 to limit the President’s power during times of aggression.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The actions of the executive branch during the Vietnam War showed continued encroachment upon legislative war powers, therefore, Congress passed the War Powers Act in 1973 to limit the President’s power during times of aggression.”

required_elements: “While the president has the power to counteract Congress by vetoing legislation, the veto can be overridden by a supermajority vote in each house of Congress, and the bill will become law.”
alternatives.correct_example: “While the president has the power to counteract Congress by vetoing legislation, the veto can be overridden by a supermajority vote in each house of Congress, and the bill will become law.”

required_elements: “It is clear that the executive was not meant to have too much power in foreign policy because the Constitution requires that any treaties negotiated by the president will need to be ratified by Congress as a check on presidential power.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence from the foundational documents that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):
alternatives.correct_example: “It is clear that the executive was not meant to have too much power in foreign policy because the Constitution requires that any treaties negotiated by the president will need to be ratified by Congress as a check on presidential power.” Examples of acceptable specific and relevant evidence from the foundational documents that support the claim or thesis (one example is one piece of evidence):

required_elements: “The Federalist 70 defends the idea of a single executive. A weak executive is less responsive to crises.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The Federalist 70 defends the idea of a single executive. A weak executive is less responsive to crises.”

required_elements: “Declaration of Independence: A too-powerful executive is bad and can threaten liberty.”
alternatives.correct_example: “Declaration of Independence: A too-powerful executive is bad and can threaten liberty.”

required_elements: “The Federalist 51 established a separation of powers/check and balances.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The Federalist 51 established a separation of powers/check and balances.”

common_traps: One piece must be from a foundational document listed in the prompt

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Listing evidence without explaining how it connects to the thesis does not earn this point.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The Declaration of Independence expressed concern that the king had too much power, and therefore a stronger legislative branch accountable to the people would be needed to ensure a constitutional government that respects civil liberty.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The Federalist 51 argued that there should be a balance of power between the legislative and executive branches so that one branch does not become overly powerful. This relatively equal balance of power between the branches should be maintained.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The Federalist 70 argued that a strong executive is needed to act to protect the nation which sometimes means the executive branch needs to be the most powerful branch of government.”

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim
alternatives.correct_example: “The power of the legislative branch to confirm presidential appointments provides a balance of power between the legislative branch and the executive because it prevents the president from unilaterally staffing the Cabinet who could implement laws that subvert the will of Congress.”

common_traps: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B
Simply restating evidence without reasoning does not earn this point

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it — not merely acknowledge it exists.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While some say the president should be more powerful than Congress because of national security concerns which require immediate action, a more powerful legislature would ensure that those responses are in line with the will of the people.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While some say that the legislative branch should be more powerful than the executive branch because the legislature is more closely tied to the will of the people, gridlock in the legislative branch could make fast action more difficult.”

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts/refutes it
alternatives.correct_example: “While some say that the power between the executive and legislative branches should be equal, it created an inefficient, slow system in response to problems.”

wrong_examples: “Many believe the president should be more powerful than Congress because the framers gave the president the most powers in the Constitution.” Describe an alternate perspective but do not refute, concede, or rebut that perspective
“Some would argue that the legislative branch should have more power than the executive branch because it is closer to the voice of the people.”
common_traps: Simply identifying an alternate perspective without rebutting/refuting it does not earn this point
